{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Type Hints & Static Analysis
  1.3  Project Structure
  1.4  Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
  1.5  Documentation
  1.6  Error Handling
  1.7  Performance & Idiomatic Python
  1.8  Security Best Practices

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS & PRACTICES
  2.1  Overview of Testing Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Third-Party)
  2.4  Other Frameworks (nose2, doctest, Hypothesis)
  2.5  Mocking & Patching
  2.6  Test Organization & Fixtures
  2.7  Code Coverage
  2.8  Continuous Integration Testing

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
-----------------------------------
- Indentation: Use 4 spaces per indentation level. Never mix tabs and spaces.
- Line Length: Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Blank Lines: Two blank lines before top-level functions/classes, one before
  methods inside a class.
- Imports: Each import on its own line. Group imports in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library imports
- Naming Conventions:
    - Functions, variables, methods: snake_case
    - Classes: PascalCase (CamelCase)
    - Constants: UPPER_CASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES
    - Private/internal: _leading_underscore
    - "Magic" methods: __double_underscore__ (dunder)
- Whitespace: No trailing whitespace. Spaces around operators and after commas.
- Use tools like black, autopep8, or ruff to auto-format code.

1.2 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and enable static checking:

    def greet(name: str, age: int) -> str:
        return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."

- For complex types, use the typing module:
    - List[str], Dict[str, int], Optional[str], Union[int, float]
    - Python 3.10+: list[str], dict[str, int], str | None, int | float

- Static analysis tools:
    - mypy: Static type checker
    - pyright / pylance: Fast type checker (VS Code integration)
    - ruff: Fast linter that also checks type-related issues
    - pylint: Comprehensive linter

- Run type checkers in CI to catch type errors before runtime.

1.3 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project metadata & build config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    │   └── index.md
    └── scripts/

- Use the src-layout (src/ directory) to prevent accidental imports of the
  package during development.
- Keep configuration in pyproject.toml (PEP 621) rather than setup.py/setup.cfg.
- Separate tests from source code.

1.4 VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS & DEPENDENCY MANAGEMENT
-------------------------------------------------
- Always use a virtual environment per project:
    python -m venv .venv
    source .venv/bin/activate   # Linux/macOS
    .venv\Scripts\activate      # Windows

- Modern dependency tools (choose one):
    - pip + requirements.txt (simple, widely supported)
    - Poetry: All

- Modern dependency tools (choose one):

    - PDM: PEP 621-compliant, fast resolver
    - pipenv: Pipfile-based workflow
